content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 11, 1985


Honorable Phillip Isenberg
Assemblyman
California Legislature
State Capitol, Room 2175
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-85-254

Dear Assemblyman Isenberg:

You have written requesting our advice relative to the
following set of facts and questions.

FACTS AND QUESTIONS

I am a member of the Wildlife Conservation Board.
During the past month, newspaper articles have pointed
out that the pending purchase of the Ocean Ranch by
the WCB involves an ownership interest by a state
official, George Gomes, chief deputy director of the
Department of Agriculture.

From all I know, the purchase is a legitimate state
function and a valuable addition to the state's
ownership of sensitive coastal land.

However, given the circumstances involved, I would
appreciate the Fair Political Practices Commission
considering whether there would be any impropriety in
the WCB purchasing the property for an impartially
determined price.

In addition, I would appreciate the FPPC's
consideration of whether it is appropriate for a
high-level state employee to engage in the sale of
property to state agencies.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Honorable Phillip Isenberg
December 11, 1985
Page 2


RESPONSE

     The issues which you have raised in your letter are not
addressed by the provisions of the Political Reform Act.
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  The Act addresses
conflicts of interest arising because an official participates
in governmental decisions affecting private financial
interests.  We are not in a position to comment on the possible
applicability of other state laws regarding state contracts
(Public Contract Code section 10410; Government Code Section
1090) or other types of conflicts of interest (Government Code
Section 8920(a)).  We are enclosing a copy of an Attorney
General's Opinion (53 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 163 (1970)) and an
Attorney General's advice letter on somewhat similar
questions.  From newspaper accounts it is our understanding
that the WCB has contacted the Attorney General for further
clarification on these questions.

     The various newspaper accounts raise other issues relating
to the lobbyist provisions of the Act.  Should either
Mr. Soares or Mr. Gomes wish to seek advice from the Commission
regarding their respective duties or responsibilities under the
Act, they are welcome to do so under the provisions of
Government Code Section 83114(a) or (b).  We, of course, do not
provide advice regarding past actions but do provide advice
with respect to future conduct.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert E. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:plh
Enclosure
cc:  George Gomes

California Legislature

DEC 9 10 18 AM '85

PHILLIP ISENBERG
ASSEMBLYMAN
□ STATE CAPITOL, SACRAMENTO 95814
(916) 445-1611

December 6, 1985

Mr. John Keplinger, Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Keplinger:

I am a member of the Wildlife Conservation Board. During the
past month, newspaper articles have pointed out that the pending
purchase of the Ocean Ranch by the WCB involves an ownership
interest by a state official, George Gomes, chief deputy director
of the Department of Agriculture.

From all I know, the purchase is a legitimate state function and
a valuable addition to the state's ownership of sensitive coastal
land.

However, given the circumstances involved, I would appreciate the
Fair Political Practices Commission considering whether there
would be any impropriety in the WCB purchasing the property for
an impartially determined price.

In addition, I would appreciate the FPPC's consideration of
whether it is appropriate for a high-level state employee to
engage in the sale of property to state agencies.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Sacramento Union
November 27, 1985


State land purchase
decision postponed
By ED MENDEL
SACRAMENTO UNION CAPITOL BUREAU

The State Wildlife Conservation
Board postponed a decision Tuesday
on the purchase of a 933-acre parcel
of land near Eureka bought earlier
this year by a group that includes a
state agriculture official and a lob-
byist.

Appraisers hired by the owners
and the board are $2 million apart,
and there is no agreement on a new
jointly funded appraisal that would
split the difference.

A board staff report said the
Ocean Ranch, located immediately
north of the Eel River mouth, has
one of only four known populations
of the endangered western lily. All
are on private land.

With a down payment of $200,000,
the parcel was purchased last May by
a group that includes George J. Gomes,
Department of Food and Agriculture
deputy director, and lobbyist George
M. Soares, who represents a number
of agricultural clients.

Gomes said Tuesday he will seek
an attorney's opinion about whether

he should remove himself from
issues before the department that
are being lobbied by Soares.

"I have been a public servant for
a long time and have been able to
maintain an integrity for work and
first," Gomes said. "I don't see any
problem with disagreeing or agree-
ing and being able to handle your . . .
private life separately."

Gomes and Soares said they and
two of the other partners, Brian L.
Davie and Thomas C. Grundiman,
were classmates at Cal Poly San
Luis Obispo and have remained in
touch since.

The four jointly purchased a half-
acre parcel near Pollock Pines
where they intended to build a
cabin.

Also listed as partners are two
members of Soares' law firm, Jan
L. Kahn and Richard C. Conway.

The partnership obtained an
option to purchase the property in
July 1983. In 1982, the state Coastal
Conservancy had tried to purchase
the property but lacked funds.

Gomes said Grundiman, who grew

● Please see page A10, col. 5

State land purchase
decision postponed
● From A1

up in the Eureka area, was able to
locate all of the previous owners,
more than a dozen complaints of
people who purchased the property
around the turn of the century.

"It was Grundiman that invited us
to participate, so it wasn't a decision
that I had to go in with or without
Soares," said Gomes.

Fish and Game Director Jack
Parnell, one of three members of
the Wildlife Conservation Board, has
said he will not vote on the purchase
because he served in the Food and
Agriculture deputy with Gomes
before his current appointment.

"Grundiman and Soares pretty
much handled the contracts with the
board," said Gomes. He said he was
only involved in the recent selection
of a third appraiser.

The owners obtained an appraisal
valuing the property at $3,265,000.

The board hired an appraiser who
put it at $2,241,000.

A new appraisal funded jointly by
the owners and the board is $2,663,-
000.

But the Department of General
Services, whose approval is
required, has not agreed to the new
appraisal.

"We are waiting for the data from

our appraiser to be submitted to
General Services," said W. John
Schmidt, board executive officer.

Soares said the property is about
two-thirds wetlands, has a 1½-mile
beachfront and an endangered plant
species and would be a good acquisi-
tion for the state to preserve wildlife
habitat.

"If they don't want it, all they
have to do is say no," said Soares.

"And of course, if they offer a price
that we don't like, I will be sure and
say so — and we will just pursue
other options."

He said that would include devel-
opment or a trade with the federal
government for timberland that
could be harvested.

Schmidt said the land is being
appraised with agriculture as the
"highest best use," which probably
means that development is unlikely
under existing law.

Soares said he sees nothing impro-
per about being a partner in a
business deal with a state official he
lobbies.

"I would be less than candid with
you if I did not say that in this town
perception problems can exist,"
Soares said.
